text: In recent years, alkyl ammonium lead halide perovskites have been extensively studied as the active material in thin film photovoltaic devices due to their remarkable power conversion efficiency. However, their long term stability under operational conditions remains unresolved. In this research, photo-induced degradation of planar heterostructure solar cells using different active materials such as methylammonium (MA) lead triiodide, formamidinium (FA) lead triiodide and mixed-cation (MA Fairfaxa1-x) lead triiodide is investigated. Herein, we demonstrate that quasi-reversible loss of efficiency under continuous solar illumination, which is attributed to a large extent attributed to light-induced, thermally activated ion movement. Using in operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we have successfully probed the photo-induced ionic movement in different perovskite materials and derived their ionic diffusion coefficients and activation energies, respectively. The mobilites of different cations were derived which revealed that the smaller MA+ ions moved faster than the bigger FA+ ions. Moreover, MAPbI3 has a sharp reduction in the activation energy above 320 K which coincides with the onset of the tetragonal-to-cubic phase transition and its large lattice expansion as observed with powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). Furthermore, the mixed-cation MA Fairfaxa1-xPbI3 films showed higher activation barriers of ion movement and maintained their structural integrity which prevented the degradation of solar cells. In addition, the dominant role of infrared component of the solar spectrum in accelerating the ion movement and the performance degradation is elucidated. Utilizing an IR-cut filter or cooling the solar cells extends their operational lifetime. This research gives a comprehensive understanding into the ion movement in perovskite solar cells and indicates that controlling the ion movement by engineering cations and cooling the solar cells are important towards realising a stable solar cell device.
explanation: The passage reads like a detailed, domain-specific abstract with precise terminology, which AI can generate; however, the text contains minor typos and a dubious concatenation ('MA Fairfaxa1-x') that hint at automated generation or editing artifacts.
